definition: Alchemy is the art of transformation — above all the transmutation of base metal into gold and of the body toward perfection or immortality. Historically a genuine proto-chemistry and a spiritual discipline at once.
history: Through Medieval Latin *alkimia* from Arabic *al-kīmiyāʾ* — the *al-* is the Arabic article, as in *algebra* and *alcohol* — over a disputed Greek root (perhaps *khēmia*, 'the Egyptian art'). The word records a transmission: Greek philosophy preserved in Arabic and handed back to a Europe that had forgotten it. See the full Word History.

def_sources.label: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery
def_sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

def_sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
def_sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

commentary: Alchemy has been through a historiographical revolution. The Victorian and Jungian picture split a base 'exoteric' gold-making from a noble 'spiritual' alchemy of the soul — a division that, as Lawrence Principe and William Newman argued in 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy' (2001), the sources simply do not support. Recent scholarship prefers the period term *chymistry* for a single enterprise, at once laboratory practice and natural philosophy, integral to the Scientific Revolution rather than opposed to it. Tara Nummedal's work on craft and fraud pushed the field further toward the workshop and away from the psyche.

commentary_sources.label: Lawrence Principe & William Newman, 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy'
commentary_sources.abstract: A widely cited essay (a chapter in the book Secrets of Nature) arguing that many 20th-century scholars misread old alchemy as secret spiritual symbolism, when most alchemists were actually doing hands-on laboratory work aimed at real, physical transformations of matter.

commentary_sources.label: Tara Nummedal, 'Words and Works in the History of Alchemy' (Isis, 2011)
commentary_sources.abstract: Argues that historians should study alchemy not just as ideas on a page but as something people actually did with their hands, reading, writing, and running experiments all at once in settings ranging from royal courts to artisan workshops.

commentary_sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
commentary_sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

etymology.origin: Medieval Latin alkimia ← Arabic al-kīmiyāʾ (al- ‘the’ + kīmiyā)
etymology.note: The Arabic preserves a Greek word — perhaps khēmia (‘the Egyptian art,’ from Khem, ‘the black land’) or khymeia (‘pouring, infusion’). The al- is the Arabic article, as in algebra, alkali, alcohol.
etymology.hook: The Arabic ‘al-’ that smuggled a science into Europe.

essay.title: Alchemy: the science with the Arabic article stuck on
essay.dek: Say it slowly and you can hear the seam: al-chemy, 'the-chemy,' the Arabic 'the' welded onto a Greek root.
essay.html:
Say alchemy slowly and you can hear the seam. That al- is the Arabic definite article, 'the' — the same one welded onto algebra, alkali, alcohol, algorithm. Each entered European languages through Arabic scholarship with its 'the' fused on so tightly that English speakers never noticed they were saying 'the-chemy.'

What follows is murkier. Arabic al-kīmiyāʾ takes up a Greek word, and scholars still argue which: perhaps khēmia, 'the Egyptian art,' from Khem, the 'black land' of the Nile; or khymeia, 'a pouring, an infusion.' Either way the word records a transmission — Greek natural philosophy preserved and transformed in the Arabic-speaking world, then handed back to a Europe that had forgotten it. The definitive account is in the corpus: Lawrence Principe's The Secrets of Alchemy and William Newman's work on the Latin Geber, alongside Lyndy Abraham's indispensable Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery, which glosses the whole symbolic vocabulary the game borrows.

That journey is the point, and it's fitting the word enacts it. Alchemy is the art of transmutation — of moving a substance across the boundary of its own nature — and its vocabulary is itself a great transmutation, Greek roots passed through Arabic gold and cooled into Latin. This study gives alchemy its own family: elixir and azoth (both Arabic), quintessence and vitriol (Latin), the athanor and the alembic. Trace them and you retrace how knowledge actually moved through the medieval world — never in a straight line, always with an article or two picked up along the way. (For the deeper history, cross-reference Ted's own alchemy databases and the Maier/Atalanta Fugiens materials.)

sources.label: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery (Cambridge)
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

sources.label: Lawrence M. Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

sources.label: William R. Newman, The Summa Perfectionis of Pseudo-Geber
sources.abstract: A full scholarly edition and translation of the Summa Perfectionis, the most influential alchemy textbook of the Middle Ages, paired with a study of who actually wrote it and how it shaped centuries of later alchemical thought.

sources.label: Stanton J. Linden (ed.), The Alchemy Reader: From Hermes Trismegistus to Isaac Newton
sources.abstract: A collected anthology of alchemy writings in English translation, running from the legendary founder-figure Hermes Trismegistus in antiquity all the way to Isaac Newton in the 1600s, showing how ideas about transforming matter changed over two thousand years.

sources.label: Michael Maier, Atalanta Fugiens
sources.abstract: A 1617 book of 50 alchemical emblems, each combining a strange allegorical picture, a short poem, and a piece of music, that uses the Greek myth of the racing huntress Atalanta as a coded map of the alchemical process.

flavor_snippets.html:
"It always amazes me what perfectly good things people throw away."
—Ludevic, necro-<mark>alchemist</mark>

"Where our potions and powders fail, perhaps nature will succeed."
—Reyhan, Samite <mark>Alchemist</mark>
Necro-<mark>alchemists</mark> gained popular support during the Travails, when geist-fueled weapons helped defend towns against mad cultists and Eldrazi monstrosities.

"Finally, the principles of corpse animation applied to bloodless materials!"
—Ludevic, necro-<mark>alchemist</mark>

"This is your only warning, <mark>alchemist</mark>. The secrets of the sea are not yours to behold. Lord Krothuss will not be so merciful next time."
—Runo Stromkirk

"Thought? Deceit? Progress? Now you're just making it hungry."
—Abral, kithkin <mark>alchemist</mark>

"I can think of worse ways to go. On second thought, maybe not."
—Ludevic, necro-<mark>alchemist</mark>
Persecuted <mark>alchemists</mark> fled deep into Kessig's wilds, seeking solitude to continue their ghoulish craft.

"There is no place like a rooftop laboratory in a lightning storm. It's where genius strikes."
—Renna, Selhoff <mark>alchemist</mark>

"Those who hide in hollow towers would do well to remember the rain."
—Reyhan, Samite <mark>Alchemist</mark>
The mushrooms growing in the wolves' fur possess curative properties incredible enough to tempt many <mark>alchemists</mark> into risking their lives to track one down.

"My next aerial design will use less metal."
—Hadaken, <mark>alchemist</mark> of Nephalia

mechanics_snippets.html:
<mark>Alchemist</mark>'s Greeting deals 4 damage to target creature.
Madness {1}{R} (If you discard this card, discard it into exile. When you do, cast it for its madness cost or put it into your graveyard.)

{T}, Sacrifice an artifact: Bjorna, Nightfall <mark>Alchemist</mark> deals 1 damage to target creature. Goad that creature. (Until your next turn, that creature attacks each combat if able and attacks a player other than you if able.)
